AI Customer-Support Copilot (single-agent)

Generated: 2026-07-21 11:32 UTC Methodologies: STRIDE, LINDDUN, PASTA LLM-enhanced: No (not requested)

System Description

A customer-facing support copilot: a chat UI feeds an LLM agent that retrieves from a RAG pipeline, calls tools via an MCP server, reads/writes long-term memory, and answers customers. Modelled with OWASP LLM Top 10 + Agentic threats driven by each element's security properties.

Severity Overview

Counts below are findings — threats proven by your model. Generic “standard checks” are listed lower down and not counted here.

Critical

High

Info
85 findings — 194 rule-based, 0 LLM-enhanced, 24 cross-boundary  ·  109 standard checks (not counted).

Data-flow overview

AI Customer-Support Copilot (single-agent) has 10 components and 9 data flows across 4 trust zones. Requests enter at Chat UI, data is stored in Vector Store and Tickets DB. 4 trust-boundary crossings, the highest-risk component is Support Copilot (15 critical findings).

Entry points: Chat UIData stores: Vector Store, Tickets DBExternal deps: —Boundary crossings: 4Unencrypted flows: —
FlowWhy it's riskySeverity
Customer → Chat UIcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Chat UI → Support Copilotcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
RAG Retriever → Vector Storecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Support Copilot → Tickets DBcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Risk hotspots: Support Copilot (15C/3H) · Chat UI (8C/5H) · Action Tools (3C/12H) · Tickets DB (3C/9H)

🚧 Untrusted-Input Boundary Crossings

Flows where untrusted (or less-trusted) input crosses into an internal trust zone. These are the highest-priority validation points in the system — every byte that enters here must be treated as hostile until proven otherwise.

No untrusted-input boundary crossings detected. Either there are no internal zones defined, or no flows enter them from less-trusted zones.

Findings (85)

These 85 findings are proven by your model. A further 109 standard checks — generic risks for these component types your model doesn’t yet confirm or rule out — are not counted as findings and stay hidden until you flip the “Standard checks” toggle.

All STRIDELINDDUNPASTA Off Hidden (109)
Unsandboxed tool/code execution: Action Tools
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An element that executes code or tools without a sandbox risks remote code execution and host compromise if the model is manipulated (OWASP LLM06 / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01LLM06T2

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency ↗Agentic T2 — Tool Misuse ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Customer → Chat UI
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-441 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk47/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability10/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Chat UI (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'DMZ (public-facing)' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Internet' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer → Chat UI crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Internet`.
  2. Issues requests to Chat UI that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Chat UI acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🗺Framework mapping

Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Chat UI → Support Copilot (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: jwt, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'DMZ (public-facing)' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Chat UI → Support Copilot crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  2. Issues requests to Support Copilot that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Support Copilot acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Tickets DB (database) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk47/50 · Critical
Damage10/10
Reproducibility10/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Tickets DB (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Tickets DB processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Tickets DB as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Tickets DB should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Tickets DB that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Tickets DB acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

  2. Issues requests to Vector Store that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.

Model output used without validation: Action Tools
High STRIDE · Tampering · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This element uses a model, plugin or tool from an unverified or community source, exposing it to backdoored weights, tampered dependencies and malicious plugins (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

No authorization on flow: Support Copilot → Action Tools
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An agent can call this tool with no authorization check, so a manipulated agent can trigger unauthorized actions — excessive agency via unscoped tool access (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Agent memory shared across users: Agent Memory
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Agent Memory (agent_memory) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Memory (agent_memory).

📝Description

Memory shared across users/tenants enables cross-boundary data leakage and memory poisoning, where one party's injected content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Agent Memory'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Memory via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For agent_memory, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1LLM04

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-287 standard check High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Chat UI's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Chat UI accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-287 standard check High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Chat UI's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Chat UI accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Chat UI's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Chat UI accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
Critical STRIDE · Repudiation · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-778 standard check High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Chat UI (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-319 standard check High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Chat UI via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Sensitive data exposure at rest
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-200 standard check High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Chat UI via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Application-layer DDoS
Critical STRIDE · Denial of Service · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-400 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Chat UI (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Chat UI's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Chat UI; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Customer → Chat UI
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-287 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Chat UI (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer → Chat UI crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Chat UI accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Chat UI, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Customer → Chat UI
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-20 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Chat UI (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'DMZ (public-facing)' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer → Chat UI crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Chat UI.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Chat UI processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Chat UI or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Chat UI does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Customer → Chat UI
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Chat UI (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Internet' into 'DMZ (public-facing)' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer → Chat UI crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Customer (user) CWE-287 standard check
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

preventive

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Agent memory — confirm isolation scope: Knowledge Base
Medium STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Knowledge Base (knowledge_base) CWE-200 standard check High→Medium
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Knowledge Base (knowledge_base).

📝Description

Shared agent memory/embeddings can leak across users/tenants and be poisoned so one party's content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic). Confirm the memory isolation scope.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'Knowledge Base' is a knowledge base component, which is subject to LLM04. Answer 'memory_scope' to confirm this as a finding or clear it. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Knowledge Base via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For knowledge_base, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM04T1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Untrusted grounding source: RAG Retriever
High STRIDE · Tampering · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

Grounding/RAG content from untrusted sources can carry indirect prompt injection and poisoned data that the model treats as instructions (OWASP LLM04 Data Poisoning / LLM01).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by RAG Retriever (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. RAG Retriever processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM04LLM01

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: RAG Retriever
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk34/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches RAG Retriever via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For retriever, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Vector/embedding weakness — no per-tenant access control: RAG Retriever
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches RAG Retriever via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For retriever, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Prompt injection exposure — confirm untrusted-input handling: RAG Retriever
Medium STRIDE · Tampering · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-345 standard check High→Medium
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

LLM/agent elements can be steered by attacker-controlled text placed in the model context (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection). Confirm whether this ingests untrusted content and whether prompt-injection defenses exist.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'RAG Retriever' is a retriever component, which is subject to LLM01. Answer 'ingests_untrusted_content / prompt_injection_defense' to confirm this as a finding or clear it. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by RAG Retriever (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. RAG Retriever processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM01T6

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection ↗Agentic T6 — Intent Breaking & Goal Manipulation ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Chat UI → Support Copilot
Critical STRIDE · Spoofing · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-287 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Chat UI → Support Copilot (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: jwt, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Chat UI → Support Copilot crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Support Copilot accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Support Copilot, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Chat UI → Support Copilot
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-20 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Chat UI → Support Copilot (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: jwt, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Application tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Chat UI → Support Copilot crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Support Copilot.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Support Copilot processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Support Copilot or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Support Copilot does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Chat UI → Support Copilot
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability10/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Chat UI → Support Copilot (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: jwt, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Information leaving 'DMZ (public-facing)' into 'Application tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Chat UI → Support Copilot crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Prompt injection — untrusted content, no defense: Support Copilot
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

This element ingests untrusted content into the model context with no prompt-injection defenses, so attacker-controlled text can override instructions (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Support Copilot (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Support Copilot processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Support Copilot (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Support Copilot processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: Support Copilot
Critical STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Support Copilot via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

📝Description

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Tickets DB (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Tickets DB processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Unauthorized changes to runtime config alter security behavior.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Tickets DB (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Tickets DB processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Tickets DB (database) CWE-778 standard check
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Tickets DB (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

🔎Why this fired

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive

Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive

A02A01API1

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Tickets DB (database) CWE-400 standard check
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Tickets DB (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Tickets DB's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Tickets DB; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Tickets DB (database) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk37/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from an external user by following flows.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Tickets DB as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Tickets DB should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Tickets DB accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Tickets DB, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.
