Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Tickets DB.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Tickets DB processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Tickets DB or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Tickets DB does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

A03

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Support Copilot → Tickets DB
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Tickets DB (database) CWE-200 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk38/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users9/10
Discoverability8/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application tier' into 'Data tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: RAG Retriever → Vector Store
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Vector Store (vector_db) CWE-287 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Vector Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Flow '' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Vector Store accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Vector Store, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🗺Framework mapping

A07

🔗References

A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: RAG Retriever → Vector Store
High STRIDE · Tampering · Vector Store (vector_db) CWE-20 finding ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Vector Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Vector Store.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Vector Store processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Vector Store or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Vector Store does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Vector Store (vector_db).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application tier' into 'Data tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow RAG Retriever → Vector Store crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Vector/embedding weakness — no per-tenant access control: Vector Store
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Vector Store (vector_db) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Vector Store (vector_db).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Vector Store'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Vector Store via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For vector_db, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1A01API1

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Insufficient audit logging
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-778 standard check Medium→High
▼
DREAD risk29/50 · Medium
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Critical user actions cannot be reliably attributed after the fact.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Chat UI (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Verbose error messages / stack traces
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-200 standard check Medium→High
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Error responses leak implementation details to attackers.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'webapp' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Chat UI via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Algorithmic complexity attack
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-400 finding Medium→High
▼
DREAD risk29/50 · Medium
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Crafted inputs trigger worst-case algorithm behavior (e.g., regex DoS).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is reachable from a less-trusted / public zone. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Chat UI (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Chat UI's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Chat UI; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🗺Framework mapping

A05

🔗References

A05:2021 Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
System prompt leakage — secrets/instructions extractable: Support Copilot
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding Medium→High
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

The system prompt is not hardened, so embedded instructions, credentials or business logic can be extracted via prompt injection and treated as non-secret (OWASP LLM07 System Prompt Leakage).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Support Copilot via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Large payloads or unbounded loops exhaust CPU/memory.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Tickets DB (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Tickets DB's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Tickets DB; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive

Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

This element processes personal data, bringing privacy obligations (GDPR/CCPA: minimization, consent, deletion, breach reporting).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Tickets DB'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Unsandboxed tool/code execution: Action Tools
Critical LINDDUN · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An element that executes code or tools without a sandbox risks remote code execution and host compromise if the model is manipulated (OWASP LLM06 / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01LLM06T2

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency ↗Agentic T2 — Tool Misuse ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
GDPR / CCPA gaps for personal data
Medium LINDDUN · Non-compliance · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-778 standard check High→Medium
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'llm_tool' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Action Tools via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (llm_tool).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Action Tools. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for llm_tool components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📝Description

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Model output used without validation: Action Tools
High LINDDUN · Tampering · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Model/tool supply chain — unverified provenance: Action Tools
High LINDDUN · Tampering · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This element uses a model, plugin or tool from an unverified or community source, exposing it to backdoored weights, tampered dependencies and malicious plugins (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM03A06

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM03:2025 Supply Chain ↗A06:2021 Vulnerable & Outdated Components ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
No authorization on flow: Support Copilot → Action Tools
High LINDDUN · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01API1API5

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Agent invokes tool without authorization: Support Copilot → Action Tools
High LINDDUN · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An agent can call this tool with no authorization check, so a manipulated agent can trigger unauthorized actions — excessive agency via unscoped tool access (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

🔎Why this fired

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Agent memory shared across users: Agent Memory
High LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · Agent Memory (agent_memory) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Memory (agent_memory).

📝Description

Memory shared across users/tenants enables cross-boundary data leakage and memory poisoning, where one party's injected content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Agent Memory'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Memory via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For agent_memory, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM08T1LLM04

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses ↗Agentic T1 — Memory Poisoning ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Third-party data leakage
Critical LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-200 finding High→Critical
▼

Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

PII shared with analytics/ad SDKs without consent.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Chat UI via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Chat UI (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Customer → Chat UI crosses the trust boundary 'Internet' → 'DMZ (public-facing)'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Chat UI via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Cardholder data triggers PCI-DSS: scope segmentation, tokenization / no-store of PANs, and key management.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'user' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Customer via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Customer. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for user components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'knowledge_base' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

detective

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Agent memory — confirm isolation scope: Knowledge Base
Medium LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · Knowledge Base (knowledge_base) CWE-200 standard check High→Medium
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Knowledge Base (knowledge_base).

📝Description

Shared agent memory/embeddings can leak across users/tenants and be poisoned so one party's content influences another's session (OWASP LLM / Agentic). Confirm the memory isolation scope.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'Knowledge Base' is a knowledge base component, which is subject to LLM04. Answer 'memory_scope' to confirm this as a finding or clear it. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Knowledge Base via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For knowledge_base, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM04T1

🔗References

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Untrusted grounding source: RAG Retriever
High LINDDUN · Tampering · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk33/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

Grounding/RAG content from untrusted sources can carry indirect prompt injection and poisoned data that the model treats as instructions (OWASP LLM04 Data Poisoning / LLM01).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by RAG Retriever (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. RAG Retriever processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM04LLM01

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning ↗LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: RAG Retriever
High LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk34/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches RAG Retriever via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For retriever, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Vector/embedding weakness — no per-tenant access control: RAG Retriever
High LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · RAG Retriever (retriever) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'RAG Retriever'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches RAG Retriever via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For retriever, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component RAG Retriever (retriever).

📝Description

LLM/agent elements can be steered by attacker-controlled text placed in the model context (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection). Confirm whether this ingests untrusted content and whether prompt-injection defenses exist.

🔎Why this fired

Standard check: 'RAG Retriever' is a retriever component, which is subject to LLM01. Answer 'ingests_untrusted_content / prompt_injection_defense' to confirm this as a finding or clear it. · severity lowered: internal, encrypted path with no model evidence of exposure

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by RAG Retriever (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. RAG Retriever processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Chat UI → Support Copilot
Critical LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding High→Critical ⚡ cross-zone
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users10/10
Discoverability9/10
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Chat UI → Support Copilot (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: jwt, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Chat UI → Support Copilot crosses the trust boundary 'DMZ (public-facing)' → 'Application tier'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Support Copilot via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Prompt injection — untrusted content, no defense: Support Copilot
Critical LINDDUN · Tampering · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk41/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

This element ingests untrusted content into the model context with no prompt-injection defenses, so attacker-controlled text can override instructions (OWASP LLM01 Prompt Injection).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Support Copilot (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Support Copilot processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM01T6

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection ↗Agentic T6 — Intent Breaking & Goal Manipulation ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Model output used without validation: Support Copilot
Critical LINDDUN · Tampering · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-345 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk39/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Support Copilot (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Support Copilot processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Sensitive information disclosure — model output not filtered: Support Copilot
Critical LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding High→Critical
▼
DREAD risk40/50 · Critical
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users8/10
Discoverability10/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

Model responses are returned without filtering or redaction, so training data, context, secrets or other users' data can leak into output (OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Support Copilot via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Re-identification of anonymized data
High LINDDUN · Identifiability · Tickets DB (database) CWE-359 finding
▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Quasi-identifiers (zip, DOB, gender) re-identify users in 'anonymous' exports.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Tickets DB (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Tickets DB. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗

preventive

▼
DREAD risk35/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

🔎Why this fired

Baseline check for a 'database' element — the model shows no specific evidence this applies here; kept for completeness, not dropped.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Tickets DB via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (database).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Tickets DB. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

On the data flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: flow Support Copilot → Tickets DB crosses the trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Vector/embedding weakness — no per-tenant access control: Vector Store
High LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · Vector Store (vector_db) CWE-200 finding
▼
DREAD risk36/50 · High
Damage8/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Vector Store (vector_db).

📝Description

The vector store enforces no per-tenant access control, so retrieval can return another tenant's embeddings and is open to embedding inversion and poisoning (OWASP LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Vector Store'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Vector Store via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For vector_db, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Chat UI. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
System prompt leakage — secrets/instructions extractable: Support Copilot
High LINDDUN · Information Disclosure · Support Copilot (ai_agent) CWE-200 finding Medium→High
▼
DREAD risk30/50 · High
Damage5/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users6/10
Discoverability8/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Support Copilot (ai_agent).

📝Description

The system prompt is not hardened, so embedded instructions, credentials or business logic can be extracted via prompt injection and treated as non-secret (OWASP LLM07 System Prompt Leakage).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Support Copilot'. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Support Copilot via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For ai_agent, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🗺Framework mapping

A02LLM07

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Cross-service user linkability
Medium LINDDUN · Linkability · Tickets DB (database) CWE-359 finding
▼
DREAD risk25/50 · Medium
Damage6/10
Reproducibility4/10
Exploitability5/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

Identifiers (emails, device IDs) let separate datasets be joined to profile a user.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Tickets DB (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Tickets DB. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗

📝Description

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Exploitability5/10
Affected users5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Tickets DB (database).

📝Description

This element processes personal data, bringing privacy obligations (GDPR/CCPA: minimization, consent, deletion, breach reporting).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Tickets DB'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Tickets DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02

🔗References

A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Forced attribution of sensitive actions
Medium LINDDUN · Non-repudiation · Chat UI (webapp) CWE-778 finding Low→Medium
▼
DREAD risk19/50 · Low
Damage3/10
Reproducibility2/10
Exploitability5/10
Affected users4/10
Discoverability5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Chat UI (webapp).

📝Description

User cannot deny a sensitive action even when they should have plausible deniability.

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: this element is tagged as handling sensitive data. · severity raised: exposed to a less-trusted zone with sensitive data

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Chat UI (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🗺Framework mapping

A09

🔗References

A09:2021 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unsandboxed tool/code execution: Action Tools
Critical PASTA · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk42/50 · Critical
Damage9/10
Reproducibility8/10
Exploitability9/10
Affected users7/10
Discoverability9/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An element that executes code or tools without a sandbox risks remote code execution and host compromise if the model is manipulated (OWASP LLM06 / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

A01LLM06T2

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency ↗Agentic T2 — Tool Misuse ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Model output used without validation: Action Tools
High PASTA · Tampering · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-345 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

Model output is consumed downstream without validation or encoding, enabling insecure output handling — injection into tools, code, SQL, or the browser (OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🗺Framework mapping

A03LLM05

🔗References

A03:2021 Injection ↗LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This element uses a model, plugin or tool from an unverified or community source, exposing it to backdoored weights, tampered dependencies and malicious plugins (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools'.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Action Tools (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Action Tools processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

No authorization on flow: Support Copilot → Action Tools
High PASTA · Elevation of Privilege · Action Tools (llm_tool) CWE-269 finding
▼
DREAD risk32/50 · High
Damage7/10
Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10
Discoverability7/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

This call may authenticate the caller but enforces no authorization, so any authenticated caller can invoke it — Broken Access Control / BOLA (OWASP A01 / API1).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🗺Framework mapping

🔗References

A01:2021 Broken Access Control ↗API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization ↗API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗

DREAD risk32/50 · High

Reproducibility6/10
Exploitability7/10
Affected users5/10

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Support Copilot → Action Tools (label: *—*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes). The receiving component is Action Tools (llm_tool).

📝Description

An agent can call this tool with no authorization check, so a manipulated agent can trigger unauthorized actions — excessive agency via unscoped tool access (OWASP LLM / Agentic).

🔎Why this fired

Evidenced: triggered by a security property you answered on 'Action Tools' for this flow.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Action Tools as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Action Tools should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.
